elements.sent-2411-001: We initiate the study of differentially private learning in the proportional dimensionality regime, in which the number of data samples $n$ and problem dimension $d$ approach infinity at rates proportional to one another, meaning that $d / n \to \delta$ as $n \to \infty$ for an arbitrary, given constant $\delta \in (0, \infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-002: This setting is significantly more challenging than that of all prior theoretical work in high-dimensional differentially private learning, which, despite the name, has assumed that $\delta = 0$ or is sufficiently small for problems of sample complexity $O(d)$, a regime typically considered ``low-dimensional'' or ``classical'' by modern standards in high-dimensional statistics.
elements.sent-2411-003: We provide sharp theoretical estimates of the error of several well-studied differentially private algorithms for robust linear regression and logistic regression, including output perturbation, objective perturbation, and noisy stochastic gradient descent, in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-004: The $1 + o(1)$ factor precision of our error estimates enables a far more nuanced understanding of the price of privacy of these algorithms than that afforded by existing, coarser analyses, which are essentially vacuous in the regime we consider.
elements.sent-2411-005: We incorporate several probabilistic tools that have not previously been used to analyze differentially private learning algorithms, such as a modern Gaussian comparison inequality and recent universality laws with origins in statistical physics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Over the last two decades, a groundbreaking line of research has demonstrated the limitations of classical statistical theory for modern high-dimensional learning problems.
elements.sent-2411-007: This vibrant area of research dates to the seminal works of \cite{donoho2009message,donoho2011noise,bayati2011amp,bayati2011lasso,el2013robust,karoui2013asymptotic ; more recent contributions may be found in \cite{sur2019modern,candes2020phase,han2023universality,wang2024universality,han2024entrywise .}}
elements.sent-2411-008: To give one example, Sur and Cand\`{e s ~\cite{sur2019modern showed that the classical theory for logistic regression can fail dramatically when applied in the proportional dimensionality regime, in which the number data samples $n$ and problem dimension $d$ approach infinity at rates proportional to one another, meaning that $d / n \to \delta$ as $n \to \infty$ for an arbitrary, given constant $\delta \in (0, \infty)$.}}
elements.sent-2411-009: In particular, while the classical theory predicts that the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) is asymptotically unbiased with variance given by the inverse Fisher information, when, say, $n \ge 5d$ or $n \ge 10d$, the MLE can exhibit nonzero bias, and greater variance than predicted by the classical theory.
elements.sent-2411-010: In a result we find particularly intriguing, they demonstrated a threshold for the dimensionality ratio $d/n$ at which the very existence of the MLE undergoes a sharp phase transition.
elements.sent-2411-011: Inspired by this body of work, we initiate the study of differentially private learning in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-012: As an illustrative example, consider the task of fitting a $d$-dimensional linear regression model to $n$ labeled data samples $(\bm{x _i, y_i) \in \R^{d \times \R$.}}

elements.sent-2411-027: In this work, we carry out the first tight utility analyses of differentially private learning algorithms in the proportional dimensionality regime, where constants start to matter and order-wise bounds cease to be meaningful.
elements.sent-2411-028: We determine the utility, including the optimal constants, of three prominent differentially private estimation algorithms---output perturbation \cite{dwork2006dp, chaudhuri2011differentially , objective perturbation \cite{chaudhuri2011differentially, kifer2012private , and noisy stochastic gradient descent (DP-SGD) \cite{song2013dpsgd, bassily2014erm ---when applied to robust linear regression and logistic regression tasks.}}}
elements.sent-2411-029: Our error bounds are tight up to a $1 \pm n^{-\Omega(1)}$ factor for robust linear regression and a $1 \pm e^{-(\log n)^{\Omega(1) }$ factor for logistic regression.}
elements.sent-2411-030: Our bounds depend in nuanced ways on the problem parameters, such as $\pe$, $\pd$, and the dimensionality ratio $\delta$.
elements.sent-2411-031: We summarize these results in \cref{sec:results and preview our techniques in \cref{sec:techniques .}}
elements.sent-2411-032: In the case of logistic regression, our main result for the (differentially private) objective perturbation algorithm closely parallels the breakthrough result of Sur and Cand\`{e s \cite{sur2019modern for the (non-private) maximum likelihood estimator.}}
elements.sent-2411-033: In that work, the authors supplanted the classical theory with a certain equation in $\R^3$, and the equation's solution characterizes the behavior of the MLE in the proportional regime (when it exists) more accurately than the Fisher information does.
elements.sent-2411-034: Our work similarly derives an equation in $\R^3$, now depending on the algorithm designer's chosen magnitude $\nu > 0$ of noise for privacy, and its solution now characterizes the utility of the objective perturbation algorithm in the proportional regime, for a wide range of notions of utility.
elements.sent-2411-035: If one lets $\nu \to 0$, the objective perturbation algorithm's output coincides with the MLE (when it exists), and, as expected, our equations characterizing $\bm{\hb}$ simplify to those of the non-private case.
elements.sent-2411-036: The contributions of this paper are summarized as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-037: We prove the first tight utility bounds for differentially private algorithms in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-038: We derive bounds for three commonly used algorithms: objective perturbation (Sections \ref{sec:objective-perturbation-linear and \ref{sec:objective-perturbation-logistic ), output perturbation (\cref{sec:output-perturbation ), and DP-SGD (\cref{sec:dp-sgd ).}}}}
elements.sent-2411-039: We introduce powerful techniques from the high-dimensional statistics literature that have not previously been used to analyze differentially private learning algorithms, which could be useful for many tasks in private high-dimensional statistics and machine learning beyond the ones we consider.
elements.sent-2411-041: The Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem (CGMT), due to \cite{stojnic2013framework, thrampoulidis2015regularized .}
elements.sent-2411-042: This theorem relates the value of a certain min-max optimization problem defined in terms of a Gaussian random matrix in $\R^{n \times d}$ to the value of a simpler problem defined in terms of two Gaussian random vectors in $\R^n$ and $\R^d$.
elements.sent-2411-043: Several of our utility analyses involve reformulating a differentially private algorithm as a min-max optimization problem, and then applying CGMT to achieve tight control of the algorithm's estimation error and, by duality, its residuals on the training data.
elements.sent-2411-044: Importantly, our proofs differ from prior CGMT analyses of non-private estimators in several ways to account for the noise introduced for differential privacy.
elements.sent-2411-045: Recent, rigorous formulations of the universality principle from statistical physics, which asserts that certain global properties of a dynamical system should be independent of its local details.
elements.sent-2411-046: We use universality to circumvent a weakness of CGMT: it requires Gaussian data.
elements.sent-2411-047: Gaussianity is incompatible with the private algorithms we consider, which all either require bounded data or enforce boundedness via feature- or gradient-clipping.
elements.sent-2411-048: Using universality, we extend our CGMT analyses to a broad class of non-Gaussian data distributions.
elements.sent-2411-049: Indeed, a major part of our proofs is verifying that the differentially private algorithms of interest to us indeed satisfy the various technical requirements needed to apply universality---specifically, the universality laws of \cite{han2023universality, han2024entrywise , which were developed to study non-private estimators.}
elements.sent-2411-050: Combining our privacy bound with our tight utility bounds leads to the first meaningful privacy-utility tradeoffs for output perturbation, objective perturbation, and DP-SGD in the proportional dimensionality regime.. \end{enumerate.}
elements.sent-2411-051: Our first technical result is a sharp utility analysis of the classic objective perturbation algorithm---and the first such analysis to provide any guarantees at all in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-052: Objective perturbation is one of the most well-known differentially private empirical risk minimization algorithms, along with output perturbation and noisy stochastic gradient descent.
elements.sent-2411-053: Objective perturbation for risk minimization was introduced in the case of pure differential privacy by \cite{chaudhuri2011differentially , extended to the case of approximate differential privacy by \cite{kifer2012private , and studied further in several subsequent theoretical and empirical works \cite{jain2014risk, iyengar2019convex, neel2020oracle, redberg2023improving .}}}
elements.sent-2411-054: For an informal description of the algorithm, suppose we are given a loss function $\ell(\bm{\beta ; (\bm{x , y))$ and wish to select $\bm{\beta \in \R^d$ to minimize the average loss on a given data set of $n$ labeled points \((\bm{x _1, y_1), \ldots, (\bm{x _n, y_n) \in \R^d \times \R.\) The objective perturbation algorithm computes the minimizer $\widehat{\bm{\beta }$ of a regularized and randomly perturbed version of the standard objective function for empirical risk minimization: \begin{equation}\widehat{\beta} = \argmin_{\beta \in \R^d}\, \sum_{i=1}^n \ell(\beta; (x_i, y_i)) + \lambda \cdot \frac{1}{2}\norm{\beta}^2 + \nu\langle \xi, \beta\rangle.\end{equation}.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-055: The effect of the quadratic regularization term, $\frac{1 {2}\norm{\bm{\beta ^2$, is controlled by the regularization strength $\lambda > 0$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-056: Similarly, the effect of the random linear perturbation term, $\langle \bm{\xi , \bm{\beta \rangle$ for $\bm{\xi \sim \mathcal{N (\bm{0 , \bm{I _d)$, is controlled by the perturbation strength $\nu > 0$ that ensures a certain level of differential privacy.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-057: In \cite{chaudhuri2011differentially, kifer2012private, bassily2014erm , the utility of $\widehat{\bm{\beta }$ is measured by its excess empirical risk: \begin{equation}\sum_{i=1}^n \ell(\widehat{\beta}; (x_i, y_i)) - \min_{\beta \in \R^d}\,\sum_{i=1}^n \ell(\beta; (x_i, y_i)).\end{equation}.}}

elements.paragraph-2411-002: Objective Perturbation for Robust Linear Regression.
elements.sent-2411-066: Ordinary least squares linear regression is notoriously brittle in the presence of outliers in the data, and a popular method to improve outlier robustness is to replace squared loss with the Huber loss \cite{huber1964robust , defined as \begin{equation}H_L(r) =  \frac{1}{2}r^2 if \abs{r} \le L L\abs{r}-\frac{1}{2}L^2 if  \abs{r} \ge L.\end{equation} The Huber loss function coincides with the squared loss when the residual $r = y - \langle \bm{x , \bm{\beta}\rangle$ is sufficiently small, but grows at a linear, rather than quadratic, rate when $\abs{r \ge L$ for a specified constant $L$, which also happens to be the Lipschitz constant of the function.}}}
elements.sent-2411-067: For ease of privacy analysis, we choose to study robust linear regression with Huber loss instead of traditional linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-068: In our utility analysis of objective perturbation for robust linear regression (\cref{thm:main-objective-huber-informal ), we will consider data $(\bm{X , \bm{y ) \in \R^{n \times d \times \R^n$ generated from a linear model, which means that there exists a ground-truth vector $\bm{\beta ^\star \in \R^d$ and suitably bounded error vector $\bm{\eps ^\star \in \R^n$ independent of $\bm{X}$ for which \begin{equation}y = X\beta^\star + \eps^\star.\end{equation}.}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-183: In recent years, a substantial body of work has focused on clarifying the capabilities and limitations of algorithms for differentially private regression \cite{dwork2009ptr, chaudhuri2011differentially, kifer2012private, mir2013landscape, bassily2014erm, wang2015privacy, sheffet2017ols, sheffet2019techniques, liu2022hdptr, varshney2022nearly, amin2023easy, brown2024ols, brown2024gradient .}

elements.sent-2411-195: A final remark we make is that in the DP literature, the phrase ``high-dimensional'' typically refers to any problem in which $d$ is large and the dependence of $n$ on $d$ is of interest, but in other communities, ``high-dimensional'' may refer to the proportional dimensionality regime, or the regime in which $d \gg n$ under sparsity assumptions.
elements.sent-2411-196: In order to avoid confusion in our paper, which builds on prior work in multiple communities, we have opted to consistently use the phrase ``proportional dimensionality'' regime rather than ``high-dimensional'' regime when appropriate.
elements.sent-2411-197: Throughout this paper, when working with an infinite sequence indexed by $n \in \N$, which may be a sequence of scalars, vectors, matrices, functions, or subsets of $\R^n$, we will often refer to the sequence as though it were a single object of that type.
elements.sent-2411-198: For example, when referring to the problem dimension $d$, it should be understood that $d$ is not merely a scalar, but rather a scalar sequence $d(n)$ (equivalently, $d_n$) indexed by $n$.
elements.sent-2411-199: Consequently, by the proportional dimensionality regime, we mean the setting in which \begin{equation}\lim_{n \to \infty}\frac{d(n)}{n} = \delta,\end{equation} where we denote the given dimensionality ratio by $\delta \in (0, \infty)$.
elements.sent-2411-200: In the proportional regime, any scalar sequence $f(n)$ satisfying $f(n) = \Theta(n^c)$ for a fixed constant $c > 0$ also satisfies $f(n) = \Theta(d^c)$, and vice versa.

elements.sent-2411-202: The algorithms we study in this paper all satisfy differential privacy (DP), the accepted definition of rigorous privacy protection for data analysis.
elements.sent-2411-203: We focus on the notion of approximate, or $(\eps, \delta)$-differential privacy with respect to the replace-one notion of adjacency:.
elements.sent-2411-204: We say a randomized algorithm $\mathcal{A : \mathcal{X ^n \to \mathcal{Y}$ satisfies $(\eps, \delta)$-differential privacy if for all adjacent $\bm{x , \bm{x ' \in \mathcal{X ^n$ and subsets $Y \subseteq \mathcal{Y}$, \begin{equation}\Pr[A(x) \in Y] \le e^{\eps}\Pr[A(x') \in Y] + \delta.\end{equation}.}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: We shall also require two other, related notions of differential privacy, known as R\'{enyi and zero-concentrated DP, that have been used in major real-world deployments of DP.}
elements.sent-2411-206: They are both defined in terms of the R\'{enyi divergence of order $\alpha > 1$ between two distributions $P$ and $Q$ over $\mathcal{Y}$: \begin{equation}D_\alpha(P\|Q) = \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\log \E_{y \sim P} \log \left(\frac{P(y)}{Q(y)}\right)^{\alpha - 1}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Importantly, it is known that $(\alpha, \eps)$-RDP implies $\left(\eps + \frac{\log(1/\delta) {\alpha-1 , \delta\right)$-DP for any $0 < \delta < 1$ \cite{mironov2017renyi .}}}

elements.sent-2411-383: From a quantitative perspective, comparing the predicted error in the non-private case ($\nu = 0$) to the differentially private case ($\nu > 0$) allows us to make the first ever measurements of the price of privacy of objective perturbation in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-384: However, there are also several interesting qualitative takeaways from Figure~\ref{fig:huber-objective-estimation .}
elements.sent-2411-385: For example, consider the plots on estimation error, which is closely related to the algorithm's test loss, or ability to generalize to out-of-sample data.
elements.sent-2411-386: In these plots, we see what appears to be a change in behavior around $d/n \approx 1$ (equivalently, around $n / (n + d) \approx 1/2$).
elements.sent-2411-387: On one side of this threshold, where $n > d$, the error of the objective perturbation algorithm is only mildly worse than in the non-private case, but on the other side, where $n < d$, there appears to be a dramatic deterioration, especially as $\lambda \to 0$.
elements.sent-2411-388: Interestingly, the ``spike'' in estimation error at $n = d$ in the non-private case, closely related to the famous double descent phenomenon in modern machine learning, seems to go away in the differentially private case, or at least be of less importance.
elements.sent-2411-389: Once again, there appears to be an interesting change in behavior around $d/n \approx 1$.
elements.sent-2411-390: In the non-private case, this is called the interpolation threshold, so as expected, on the side where $n < d$, the residual error is $0$ as $\lambda \to 0$, but on the side where $n > d$, the residual error assumes a small positive value, even as $\lambda \to 0$.
elements.sent-2411-391: Strikingly, in the differentially private case, we observe a spike in residual error around $d/n \approx 1$.
elements.sent-2411-392: This spike does not truly reach infinity, since truncated residual error is, by definition, capped at $L^2 = 100$, but is nevertheless qualitatively similar to what we would expect of test loss, not training loss, in the non-private case.
elements.sent-2411-393: One possible intuitive explanation for why differential privacy creates a spike in the training loss around $n = d$ draws on a line of work on adaptive data analysis introduced by \cite{dwork2015preserving, bassily2016ada .}
elements.sent-2411-394: This work rigorously showed that any differentially private learning algorithm satisfying differential privacy automatically enjoys a certain resilience against overfitting and false discovery.
elements.sent-2411-395: With this in mind, it arguably makes sense that a training loss-related metric, when applied to a differentially private algorithm, might exhibit qualitative behaviors traditionally seen only when analyzing test loss.

elements.sent-2411-576: In this section, we study the privacy-utility tradeoff for objective perturbation, applied to the problem of logistic regression.
elements.sent-2411-577: The version of the algorithm that we consider is still that of \cref{alg:objective-perturbation , but we will instantiate it with a different loss function $\ell$.}
elements.sent-2411-578: Before stating the main result of this section (\cref{thm:logistic-objective-perturbation ), we briefly review the setup for logistic regression and comment on some slight differences in the notion of convergence that will arise in the theorem's conclusion.}
elements.sent-2411-579: In the worst case, $(\bm{x _i, y_i)$ are arbitrary points in $\R^{d + 1}$ with $\norm{\bm{x _i \le R$ and $y \in \{0, 1\}$, but in the average case, there is a ground-truth coefficient vector $\bm{\beta ^\star \in \R^d$ independent of $\bm{X}$ such that \begin{equation}y|(X, \beta^\star) \sim Bernoulli(\rho'(X\beta^\star)).\end{equation}.}}}}
elements.sent-2411-580: Here, $\rho(t) = \log(1 + e^t)$, so that $\rho'(t) = 1/(1 + e^{-x )$ is the well-known sigmoid function.}
elements.sent-2411-582: The logistic loss of a coefficient vector $\bm{\beta}$ on a data point $(\bm{x , y)$ is \begin{equation}\rho(\langle x, \beta\rangle) - y\langle x, \beta\rangle =\rho(-(2y-1)\langle x, \beta\rangle),\end{equation} which simplifies to $\rho(\langle \bm{x , \bm{\beta}\rangle)$ if $y = 0$ or $\rho(-\langle \bm{x , \bm{\beta}\rangle)$ if $y = 1$.}}}

elements.sent-2411-597: Before we begin the utility proof, we give a non-rigorous, heuristic derivation to motivate the three equations in the statement of \cref{thm:logistic-objective-perturbation}\ref{thm:logistic-objective-utility .}
elements.sent-2411-598: The derivation is based on Theorem 1 of \cite{salehi2019impact , which determines the asymptotic behavior of $\widehat{\bm{\beta }$ for regularized logistic regression, provided that $\bm{\beta ^\star$ has i.i.d.}}}
elements.sent-2411-599: In order to apply Theorem 1 of \cite{salehi2019impact , one first assumes that the following system of six equations in six variables has a unique solution $(\alpha^\star, \sigma^\star, \gamma^\star, \theta^\star, \tau^\star, r^\star)$.}
elements.sent-2411-600: These equations are stated in terms of $Z, Z_1, Z_2 \iid \mathcal{N (0, 1)$ and $\bm{\beta ^\star \iid \beta^\star_0$, as follows.\footnote{We have replaced $\delta$ with $1/\delta$ and $\lambda$ with $\lambda \delta$ to account for differences in notation and scaling in \cite{salehi2019impact .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-601: Next, observe that logistic regression with objective perturbation can be recast as regularized logistic regression with a ``randomized regularizer,'' as follows.
elements.sent-2411-602: First, sample $\xi_1, \ldots, \xi_d \iid \xi_0$ where $\xi_0 \sim \mathcal{N (0, 1)$ and define the functions $f_0, \ldots, f_d : \R \to \R$ as \begin{equation}f_j(\beta) = \frac{\lambda}{2}\beta^2 + \nu \xi_j \beta.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-603: Then, the objective perturbation algorithm's output $\widehat{\bm{\beta }$ can be written as \begin{equation}\widehat{\beta} = \argmin_{\beta \in \R^d}\, \rho(X \beta) - \langle y, X\beta\rangle + \sum_{j=1}^d f_j(\beta_j).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-604: Therefore, even though Theorem 1 of \cite{salehi2019impact requires the coordinate-wise application of a single, deterministic regularizer $f$, and the above expression uses $d$ distinct, ``randomized regularizers'' $f_1, \ldots, f_d$, one might guess that substituting $f = f_0$ into the above system of six equations and taking the expectation over the randomness of $f_0$ might nevertheless lead to the equations in \cref{thm:logistic-objective-perturbation}\ref{thm:logistic-objective-utility , which we will later show correctly describe $\widehat{\bm{\beta }$.}}}
elements.sent-2411-605: Indeed, this is the case, which we now show.
elements.sent-2411-606: First, observe that \begin{equation}\label{eq:prox} \prox_{t f_0}(x) = \frac{1}{1+t} x + \frac{t}{1+t}\left(-\frac{\nu}{\lambda}\xi_0\right) = \frac{x - t \cdot \frac{\nu}{\lambda} \xi_0}{1 + t}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-608: If the random variables inside the expectation ($Z$, $\beta^\star_0$, and $\xi_0$) are all independent, we have $\E[Z\beta^\star_0] = \E[Z\xi_0] = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-610: Thus, the above equation simplifies to \begin{equation}\label{eq:tau} \gamma = \frac{\delta \sigma\tau}{1+\lambda\delta\sigma\tau}.\end{equation}.

elements.section-2411-005: Privacy of Objective Perturbation with Small \texorpdfstring{$\lambda$}
elements.sent-2411-830: The main result of this section, \cref{thm:our-objective-perturbation-privacy , is an extension of \cref{thm:redberg-privacy (Theorems 3.1 and 3.2 of \cite{redberg2023improving ).}}}
elements.sent-2411-831: While \cref{thm:our-objective-perturbation-privacy will apply to all strictly positive regularization strengths $\lambda > 0$ and perturbation magnitudes $\nu > 0$, \cref{thm:redberg-privacy required that $\lambda > s$ for some strictly positive $s$ depending on the smoothness of the loss function.}}
elements.sent-2411-832: For example, $s = 1$ in the case of robust linear regression, and $s = 1/4$ in the case of logistic regression.
elements.sent-2411-833: For this section only, we shall the refer to various privacy loss parameters of the objective perturbation algorithm by $\eps$, $\delta$, $\alpha$, and $\rho$, rather than $\pe$, $\pd$, $\pa$, and $\pr$.
elements.sent-2411-834: Suppose that $\ell_0 : \R^2 \to \R$ satisfies $\abs{\partial_1 \ell_0(\eta, y) \le L$ and $0 \le \partial_1^2 \ell_0(\eta, y) \le s$ for some constants $L, s > 0$ and for all $\eta, y \in \R$.}
elements.sent-2411-836: The algorithm also satisfies add/remove-one $(\alpha, \eps)$-R\'{e nyi differential privacy for any $\alpha > 1$ and \begin{equation}\eps = \log\left(1 + \frac{s}{\lambda}\right) + \frac{L^2}{2\nu^2} + \frac{1}{\alpha - 1}\log E_{X \sim N{\left(0, \frac{L^2}{\nu^2}\right)}}\bigl[e^{(\alpha - 1)\abs{X}}\bigr].\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-837: Before we prove \cref{thm:our-objective-perturbation-privacy , we make a couple comments.}
elements.sent-2411-838: First, the only differences between the statement of this theorem and the statement of \cref{thm:redberg-privacy are our removal of the assumption that $\lambda > s$, and, relatedly, our replacement of the quantity $- \log(1 - s / \lambda)$ in both the approximate DP and RDP bounds with the strictly smaller quantity $\log(1 + s / \lambda)$.}
elements.sent-2411-839: We also remark that the conclusion of \cref{thm:our-objective-perturbation-privacy implies a $\rho$-zCDP bound with constant $\rho$ for any $\lambda,\nu > 0$, justifying our informal commentary in \cref{sec:results :.}}
elements.sent-2411-840: Under the same conditions as \cref{thm:our-objective-perturbation-privacy , \cref{alg:objective-perturbation satisfies $\rho$-zCDP for \begin{equation}\rho = \log{\left(1 + \frac{s}{\lambda}\right)} + \frac{L^2}{2\nu^2} + \sqrt{\frac{2}{\pi}} \cdot \frac{L}{\nu}.\end{equation}.}}

elements.sent-2411-871: Suppose there exist $\sigma^\star, \tau^\star > 0$ solving the following system of two scalar equations in two variables $(\sigma, \tau)$, which we write in terms of a dummy variable $Z \sim \mathcal{N (0, 1)$ and $\kappa^2 = \E(\beta^\star_0)^2$ as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sigma^2 = \tau^2\left(\frac{1}{\delta} \E\mathopen{}\left[\frac{\sigma Z + \varepsilon^\star_0}{1 + \tau}\right]_L^2 + \lambda^2\kappa^2\right), \label{eq:huber-1-out} \tau = \frac{1}{\lambda \delta}\left(\delta - \frac{\tau}{1+\tau}\Pr\mathopen{}\left[-L < \frac{\sigma Z + \varepsilon^\star_0}{1 + \tau} < L\right]\right). \label{eq:huber-2-out}\end{equation} \end{subequations Then, in terms of $(\sigma^\star, \tau^\star)$, the estimation error $\widehat{\bm{\beta - \bm{\beta ^\star$ satisfies, for $\xi_0 \sim \mathcal{N (0, 1)$, \begin{equation}(\beta^\star, \, \xi, \, \widehat{\beta} - \beta^\star) \rightsquigarrow \left(\beta_0^\star, \; \xi_0, \; \tau^\star{\left(\sqrt{\frac{1}{\delta} \E\mathopen{}\left[\frac{\sigma^\star Z + \varepsilon^\star_0}{1 + \tau^\star}\right]_L^2} Z - \lambda \beta^\star_0\right)} + \nu\xi_0\right).\end{equation}. \end{enumerate.}}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-874: Suppose there are unique $\sigma^\star, \alpha^\star, \gamma^\star > 0$ solving the following system of three scalar equations in three variables $(\sigma, \alpha, \gamma)$, which we write in terms of dummy variables $Z_1, Z_2 \iid \mathcal{N (0, 1)$ as \begin{subequations \begin{equation}\sigma^2 = \frac{\gamma^2}{\delta}\E\mathopen{}\left[2\rho'(-\kappa Z_1)\rho'(\prox_{\gamma\rho}(\kappa\alpha Z_1+\sigma Z_2) \big)^2\right], \label{eq:logistic-1-out} \alpha = -\frac{1}{\delta}\E[ 2\rho''(-\kappa Z_1)\prox_{\gamma \rho}\big(\kappa \alpha Z_1+\sigma Z_2\big)], \label{eq:logistic-2-out} \gamma = \frac{1}{\lambda \delta}\mathopen{}\left(\delta - 1 + \E\mathopen{}\left[\frac{2\rho'(-\kappa Z_1)}{1+\gamma \rho''\big(\prox_{\gamma\rho}(\kappa\alpha Z_1 + \sigma Z_2)\big)}\right]\right). \label{eq:logistic-3-out}\end{equation} \end{subequations Then, the estimation error $\widehat{\bm{\beta - \bm{\beta ^\star$ satisfies \begin{equation}(\beta^\star, \, \xi, \, \widehat{\beta}) \dashrightarrow \left(\beta^\star_0, \, \xi_0, \, \alpha^\star \beta^\star_0 + \sigma^\star Z + \nu \xi_0\right).\end{equation}. \end{enumerate.}}}}}}}

elements.sent-2411-904: Using our estimates, we discover a previously unobserved ``double descent''-like phenomenon in the training error of objective perturbation for robust linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-905: We also identify settings in which output perturbation outperforms objective perturbation on average, and vice versa, demonstrating that the relative performance of these algorithms is less clear-cut than suggested by prior work.
elements.sent-2411-906: To prove our main theorems, we introduce several probabilistic tools that have not previously been used to analyze differentially private learning algorithms, such as a modern Gaussian comparison inequality and recent universality laws with origins in statistical physics.
elements.sent-2411-907: Over the last two decades, a groundbreaking line of research has demonstrated the limitations of classical statistical theory for modern high-dimensional learning problems \cite{donoho2009message,donoho2011noise,bayati2011amp,bayati2011lasso,el2013robust,karoui2013asymptotic, sur2019modern,candes2020phase,han2023universality,wang2024universality,han2024entrywise .}
elements.sent-2411-908: For example, although the classical theory of logistic regression predicts that the maximum likelihood estimator (MLE) is asymptotically unbiased with variance given by the inverse Fisher information, these predictions fail dramatically in the proportional dimensionality regime, in which the number data samples $n$ and problem dimension $d$ approach infinity at rates proportional to one another.
elements.sent-2411-909: Indeed, \cite{sur2019modern showed that even if $n = 5d$ or $n = 10d$, the MLE can be biased, with greater variance than predicted by the classical theory.}
elements.sent-2411-910: In a result we find particularly intriguing, they further show that the MLE fails to exist with high probability when the dimensionality ratio $d/n$ crosses a certain phase transition threshold.
elements.sent-2411-911: Despite these challenges, this proportional high-dimensional regime is highly relevant in modern data science, where technological advancements generate datasets with feature dimensions comparable to, or even exceeding, sample sizes.
elements.sent-2411-912: Motivated by these insights and the growing importance of privacy-preserving learning, we initiate the study of differentially private learning in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-913: In this setting, it is known that there exists an $(\pe, \pd)$-differentially private algorithm, due to \cite{liu2022hdptr , such that for any sufficiently small $\alpha > 0$, the algorithm's output $\widehat{\bm{\beta }$ has error\footnote{The factor of $1/d$ in the estimation error is appropriate because the $\bm{x _i$ have covariance $\frac{1 {d}\bm{I _d$.}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-914: Therefore, the above-mentioned upper bound leaves unanswered the question of whether the algorithm in question exhibits a nontrivial privacy-utility tradeoff in the proportional regime, unless the constant $\delta$ to which $d/n$ converges is sufficiently small, a regime considered ``low-dimensional'' or ``classical'' by modern standards \cite{wainwright2019statistics .}
elements.sent-2411-915: Instead, a more refined approach, relying on the solution to a system of nonlinear equations, is necessary to capture the nuanced dependence on $d/n$ in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-916: Our first main contribution is the first tight utility analysis of several differentially private learning algorithms in the proportional dimensionality regime.
elements.sent-2411-917: We determine the utility, including the optimal constants, of three prominent private estimation algorithms:.
elements.sent-2411-918: In Sections~\ref{sec:main-body-objective-perturbation-linear and \ref{sec:main-body-objective-perturbation-logistic , we determine the error of the objective perturbation algorithm, due to \cite{chaudhuri2011differentially, kifer2012private .}}}
elements.sent-2411-919: Our bounds are tight up to a $1 \pm n^{-\Omega(1)}$ factor for robust linear regression and a $1 \pm e^{-(\log n)^{\Omega(1) }$ factor for logistic regression, and they depend in nuanced ways on the problem parameters, such as $\pe$, $\pd$, and the dimensionality ratio $\delta$.}
elements.sent-2411-920: Using these estimates, we discover a previously unobserved ``double descent''-like phenomenon in the training error of objective perturbation for robust linear regression, which is distinct from the better-known double descent curve for the test error of non-private linear regression.
elements.sent-2411-921: In \cref{sec:main-body-privacy , we also give a refined privacy bound for the objective perturbation algorithm, which extends the analysis of \cite{redberg2023improving to arbitrarily small regularization strengths $\lambda > 0$ and perturbation magnitudes $\nu > 0$.}}
elements.sent-2411-922: In \cref{sec:main-body-output-perturbation , we determine similarly tight error estimates for the output perturbation algorithm, due to \cite{dwork2006dp, chaudhuri2011differentially .}}
elements.sent-2411-923: Using our estimates, we show that the relative performance of objective perturbation and output perturbation is more nuanced than suggested by previous work.
elements.sent-2411-924: Indeed, while some prior analyses \cite{chaudhuri2011differentially, kifer2012private suggest that objective perturbation is better overall, we identify some parameter settings for which output perturbation does better on average.}
elements.sent-2411-925: In \cref{sec:main-body-dp-sgd , we determine the error of a version of the noisy stochastic gradient descent algorithm (DP-SGD) of \cite{song2013dpsgd, bassily2014erm that is restricted to a constant number of iterations.}}
elements.sent-2411-926: These estimates are accurate up to a $1 + o(1)$ factor, and they apply to certain variants of robust linear regression and logistic regression.
elements.sent-2411-927: Our second main contribution is the introduction of two powerful techniques from the high-dimensional statistics literature that have not previously been used to analyze differentially private learning algorithms.
elements.sent-2411-928: The first technique is the Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem (CGMT) of \cite{stojnic2013framework, thrampoulidis2015regularized , which relates certain min-max optimization problems defined in terms of Gaussian random matrices in $\R^{n \times d}$ to simpler problems defined in terms of Gaussian random vectors in $\R^n$ and $\R^d$.}
elements.sent-2411-929: The second technique is the universality law of \cite{han2023universality, han2024entrywise , which allow us to apply CGMT to a wide range of non-Gaussian data distributions, including bounded data arising from the feature- or gradient-clipping subroutines of differentially private algorithms.}
elements.sent-2411-930: Both CGMT and universality were developed to analyze non-private estimators, and a major part of our proofs, which we sketch in \cref{sec:techniques , is verifying that the differentially private algorithms of interest to us satisfy the technical requirements needed to apply these techniques.}

elements.figure-2411-009: \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/huber_output/output_estimation_errors_no_noise.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/huber_output/output_estimation_errors_pt5.pdf} (a) \cref{alg:output-perturbation} with Huber loss ($L = 10$) and $\bm{\eps}^\star \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, (1/5)^2\bm{I}_n)$ and $\bm{y} = \bm{X}\bm{\beta}^\star + \bm{\eps}^\star$. \label{fig:huber-output-estimation} \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/logistic_output/logistic_output_estimation_errors_no_noise.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/logistic_output/logistic_output_estimation_errors_pt5.pdf} (b) \cref{alg:output-perturbation} with logistic loss and $\bm{y} \sim \mathrm{Bernoulli}(\rho'(\bm{X}\bm{\beta}^\star))$. \label{fig:logsitic-output-estimation} \caption{Predictions of Corollaries \ref{thm:main-huber-output-perturbation} and \ref{thm:logistic-output-perturbation} on the estimation error of \cref{alg:output-perturbation}. In all plots, curves correspond to theoretical predictions, and dots correspond to the mean over $100$ simulations of the algorithm on synthetic data with $n \times d = 1000$. In the left plots, the perturbation magnitude is $\nu = 0$, but in the right plots, $\nu = 1/2$. In all plots, the signal strength is $\kappa = 1$, and we consider $\bm{\beta}^\star \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \kappa^2\bm{I}_d)$, along with $\bm{X} \sim \frac{1}{\sqrt{d}} \mathrm{Uniform}(\{-1,+1\}^{n \times d})$.} \label{fig:output-estimation}
elements.section-2411-011: Utility of Noisy Stochastic Gradient Descent
elements.figure-2411-010: \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/dp_sgd/huber_ce_descent_estimation_errors_no_noise_step_0pt5.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/dp_sgd/huber_ce_descent_estimation_errors_1pt0_step_0pt5.pdf} (a) \cref{alg:dp-sgd} with Huber loss, ``conditional expectation'' version ($L = 10$, $\eps^\star_0 \sim \mathcal{N}(0, (1/5)^2)$). \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/dp_sgd/logistic_ce_descent_estimation_errors_no_noise.pdf} \includegraphics[width=0.495\textwidth]{figures/dp_sgd/logistic_ce_descent_estimation_errors_1pt0.pdf} (b) \cref{alg:dp-sgd} with logistic loss, ``conditional expectation'' version. \caption{Predictions of Theorems \ref{thm:dp-sgd-huber} and \ref{thm:dp-sgd-logistic} on the estimation error of \cref{alg:dp-sgd}. In all plots, curves correspond to theoretical predictions, and dots correspond to the mean over $10^4$ simulations of the algorithm on synthetic data with $n \times d = 1000$. In the left plots, the noise magnitude is $\nu = 0$, but in the right plots, $\nu = 1/10$. In all plots, the signal strength is $\kappa = 1$, the step size is $\gamma = 1/2 \cdot 1/(1+\delta)$, and we consider $\bm{\beta}^\star \sim \mathcal{N}(\bm{0}, \kappa^2\bm{I}_d)$, along with $\bm{X} \sim \frac{1}{\sqrt{d}} \mathrm{Uniform}(\{-1,+1\}^{n \times d})$ and $\bm{y} = \bm{X}\bm{\beta}^\star$.} \label{fig:dp-sgd}
elements.sent-2411-1063: The random variables are {\allowdisplaybreaks\begin{equation}\theta^{(0)} = \left[{c} 0 \beta^\star_0 \right] \in \R^2, \theta^{(t + 1)} = (1 + \Gamma^{(t)})\theta^{(t)} - \gamma \nu \left[{c} \xi^{(t)}_0 0\right] + \sum_{k=0}^{t - 1}R_g(t, k)\theta^{(k)} + u^{(t)} \in \R^2, \eta^{(t)} = -\gamma\sum_{k=0}^{t-1} R_\theta(t, k) \left[{c} \E_{\eps^\star_0}[\eta^{(k)}_1 - \eta^{(k)}_2 - \eps^\star_0]_L 0 \right] + \omega^{(t)} \in \R^2, \frac{\partial \eta^{(t)}}{\partial \omega^{(s)}} = \left\{{ll} 0 if  t < s, I_2 if  t = s, -\gamma\sum_{k=0}^{t-1}R_\theta(t, k)\Pr_{\eps^\star_0}\left[\abs{\eta^{(k)}_1 - \eta^{(k)}_2 - \eps^\star_0} < L\right]\left[{cc} 1 -1 0 0 \right] \frac{\partial \eta^{(k)}}{\partial \omega^{(s)}} if  t > s \right\} \in \R^{2 \times 2},\end{equation} where $\xi^{(0) _0, \ldots, \xi^{(T) _0 \iid \mathcal{N (0, 1)$, the random vector $[(\bm{u ^{(0) )^\top \mid \cdots \mid (\bm{u ^{(t) )^\top]^\top \in \R^{2(t + 1)}$ (for each $t = 0, \ldots, T$) follows a centered multivariate Gaussian distribution with covariance matrix entries given by $\bm{C_g}$, and the random vector $[(\bm{\omega ^{(0) )^\top \mid \cdots \mid (\bm{\omega ^{(t) )^\top]^\top \in \R^{2(t + 1)}$ (for each $t = 0, \ldots, T$) follows a centered multivariate Gaussian distribution with covariance matrix entries given by $\bm{C_\theta}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1064: The deterministic $2 \times 2$ matrices are {\allowdisplaybreaks\begin{equation}R_\theta(t + 1, s) = \left\{{ll} 0 if t < s, I_2 if t = s, (I_2 + \Gamma^{(t)})R_\theta(t, s) + \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} R_g(t, k)R_\theta(k, s) if t > s \right\} \in \R^{2 \times 2} R_g(t, s) = -\frac{\gamma}{\delta}\E\left[\Pr_{\eps^\star_0}\left[\abs{\eta^{(t)}_1 - \eta^{(t)}_2 - \eps^\star_0} < L\right]\left[{cc} 1 -1 0 0 \right] \frac{\partial\eta^{(t)}}{\partial \omega^{(s)}}\right] \in \R^{2 \times 2}, \Gamma^{(t)} = -\frac{\gamma}{\delta}\E\left[\Pr_{\eps^\star_0}\left[\abs{\eta^{(t)}_1 - \eta^{(t)}_2 - \eps^\star_0} < L\right]\left[{cc} 1 -1 0 0 \right]\right] \in \R^{2 \times 2}, C_\theta(t, s) = \E[\theta^{(t)}(\theta^{(s)})^\top] \in \R^{2 \times 2}, C_g(t, s) = \frac{\gamma^2}{\delta}\E\left[{cc} \E_{\eps^\star_0}[\eta^{(t)}_1 - \eta^{(t)}_2 - \eps^\star_0]_L\E_{\eps^\star_0}[\eta^{(s)}_1 - \eta^{(s)}_2 - \eps^\star_0]_L 0 0 0 \right] \in \R^{2 \times 2}.\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-1065: The random variables are {\allowdisplaybreaks\begin{equation}\theta^{(0)} = \left[{c} 0 \beta^\star_0 \right] \in \R^2, \theta^{(t + 1)} = (1 + \Gamma^{(t)})\theta^{(t)} - \gamma \nu \left[{c} \xi^{(t)}_0 0\right] + \sum_{k=0}^{t - 1}R_g(t, k)\theta^{(k)} + u^{(t)} \in \R^2, \eta^{(t)} = -\gamma \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} R_\theta(t, k) \left[{c} \rho'(\eta^{(k)}_1)\rho'(-\eta^{(k)}_2) - \rho'(-\eta^{(k)}_1)\rho'(\eta^{(k)}_2) 0 \right] + \omega^{(t)} \in \R^2, \frac{\partial \eta^{(t)}}{\partial \omega^{(s)}} = \left\{{ll} 0 if  t < s, I_2 if  t = s, -\gamma \sum_{k=0}^{t-1}R_\theta(t, k)\left[{cc} \rho''(\eta^{(k)}_1) -\rho''(\eta^{(k)}_2) 0 0 \right] \frac{\partial \eta^{(k)}}{\partial \omega^{(s)}} if  t > s \right\} \in \R^{2 \times 2},\end{equation} where $\xi^{(0) _0, \ldots, \xi^{(T) _0 \iid \mathcal{N (0, 1)$, the random vector $[(\bm{u ^{(0) )^\top \mid \cdots \mid (\bm{u ^{(t) )^\top]^\top \in \R^{2(t + 1)}$ (for each $t = 0, \ldots, T$) follows a centered multivariate Gaussian distribution with covariance matrix entries given by $\bm{C_g}$, and the random vector $[(\bm{\omega ^{(0) )^\top \mid \cdots \mid (\bm{\omega ^{(t) )^\top]^\top \in \R^{2(t + 1)}$ (for each $t = 0, \ldots, T$) follows a centered multivariate Gaussian distribution with covariance matrix entries given by $\bm{C_\theta}$.}}}}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-1066: The (deterministic / constant) $2 \times 2$ matrices are {\allowdisplaybreaks\begin{equation}R_\theta(t + 1, s) = \left\{{ll} 0 if t < s, I_2 if t = s, (I_2 + \Gamma^{(t)})R_\theta(t, s) + \sum_{k=0}^{t-1} R_g(t, k)R_\theta(k, s) if t > s \right\} \in \R^{2 \times 2} R_g(t, s) = -\frac{\gamma}{\delta}\E\left[\left[{cc} \rho''(\eta^{(t)}_1) -\rho''(\eta^{(t)}_2) 0 0 \right] \frac{\partial\eta^{(t)}}{\partial \omega^{(s)}}\right] \in \R^{2 \times 2}, \Gamma^{(t)} = -\frac{\gamma}{\delta}\E\left[{cc} \rho''(\eta^{(t)}_1) -\rho''(\eta^{(t)}_2) 0 0 \right] \in \R^{2 \times 2}, C_\theta(t, s) = \E[\theta^{(t)}(\theta^{(s)})^\top] \in \R^{2 \times 2}, C_g(t, s) = \frac{\gamma^2}{\delta}\E\left[{cc} \substack{(\rho'(\eta^{(t)}_1)\rho'(-\eta^{(t)}_2) - \rho'(-\eta^{(t)}_1)\rho'(\eta^{(t)}_2)) \times (\rho'(\eta^{(s)}_1)\rho'(-\eta^{(s)}_2) - \rho'(-\eta^{(s)}_1)\rho'(\eta^{(s)}_2))} 0 0 0 \right] \in \R^{2 \times 2}.\end{equation}.}
